summary: This notebook demonstrates how to integrate and analyze a supplemental AI-generated coverage charting dataset for the NFL Big Data Bowl 2026. It loads frame-level and player-level parquet files, calculates how defensive coverage schemes evolve from the snap to the end of a play, and joins these predictions with official tracking data. The author provides code to generate interactive Plotly bar charts and frame-by-frame animations that visualize player alignments and coverage probabilities. Readers should learn how to merge external charting data into their workflow and explore temporal defensive disguises. The notebook serves as a practical guide for exploratory analysis and visualization rather than a predictive modeling pipeline.
competition_flow: Loads supplemental frame-level and player-level coverage parquet files, analyzes coverage scheme evolution from snap to end of play, joins the data with official tracking data, and generates interactive Plotly visualizations and animations.
data_reading: Reads parquet files using Polars (pl.read_parquet) for player-play and frame-level coverage data.
Reads CSV tracking data using Polars (pl.read_csv).
data_processing: Filters data by game_id and play_id to isolate specific plays.
Casts nfl_id to string for consistent joins across datasets.
Broadcasts frame-level predictions to player-level data via left joins.
Converts Polars DataFrames to Pandas for statistical aggregation and Plotly visualization.
insights: AI-generated frame-level coverage data can effectively reveal how defensive schemes disguise and evolve from pre-snap to post-snap.
Joining supplemental charting data with official tracking data enables rich, frame-by-frame visualizations of player alignments and responsibilities.
Coverage distributions shift significantly during plays, with a notable trend toward 2-high shell coverages by the end of the play.
